lenses.guidance: 'Until Shiloh comes' (v 10) is Genesis's most contested crux — the Hebrew may read 'until tribute comes to him' or 'until he comes to whom it belongs'. The grammar's reach toward an unnamed sceptre-bearer is the prophecy's force.

Reuben loses (vv 3-4), Simeon and Levi are scattered (vv 5-7), Judah ascends to lion-status (vv 8-12) — the structure inverts birth-order with moral reckoning. Tribal destinies follow the chapters' moral arc, not the genealogy.

The 'lion's cub' of Judah (v 9) and 'the sceptre' (v 10) prefigure the Davidic monarch and beyond — the Lion of the tribe of Judah of Revelation 5:5. The chapter is the anchor pattern for two millennia of messianic reading.

Judah's blessing (vv 8-12) is the chapter's redemptive heart. The tribe that produced Judah-of-Tamar — the tribe Jacob refused to favour at its head — becomes the covenant line through which kingship and salvation arrive.

'The obedience of the peoples shall be his' (v 10) is named Christological data by Hebrews 7:14 — 'our Lord descended from Judah' — and Revelation 5:5 closes the loop. The Shiloh verse points to Jesus across a thousand years.

The Joseph blessings (vv 22-26) invoke 'the God of your father' alongside 'the Almighty' with imagery of 'everlasting hills' — a canonical bridge between patriarchal naming and Sinai's covenant titles, threading Scripture.
